methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

private.name: Fuse Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Nuclear Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M+

data.narrative:
General Matter, a Series A nuclear energy company founded by Scott Nolan and Lee Robinson, has raised $50M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a universe of 11 comparable companies—3 public (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private peers including Fuse Energy, TerraPower, X-Energy, and Kairos Power—though the nuclear energy subsector remains sparsely populated at narrow comparable bands.

Stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable approach, using the $50M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to subsequent-stage transitions. This method yields an implied valuation of $100M. Peer-median triangulation was not applicable due to insufficient narrow-stage peers meeting quality thresholds; the existing comp set lacks three or more same-stage or adjacent-stage datapoints with comparable valuation tiers. Relying on all-sector medians would introduce material noise given the specialized nature of advanced reactor development.

The triangulated valuation range of $85M to $115M (median $100M) reflects reliance on a single primary method. The range brackets Stage A step-up conventions while acknowledging early-stage nuclear technology company volatility. Key uncertainties include the narrow peer dataset—only 8 private comps across the sector—and early commercialization risk typical of frontier nuclear ventures. Additional weight of evidence from licensing progress, capital efficiency, and technical differentiation would strengthen conviction in any valuation within this band.
